```json
{
  "claims": [
    {
      "claim": "Domain-specific AI applications are finding product-market fit faster than horizontal AI, especially in legal, healthcare, and coding, with Harvey AI achieving $0 to $200M ARR in 36 months as a benchmark case study.",
      "anchor": "Domain-specific AI applications are finding product-market fit faster than horizontal AI, especially in legal, healthcare, and coding. Harvey AI's $0→$200M ARR in 36 months is the benchmark case study for how vertical AI companies win."
    },
    {
      "claim": "As of April 2026, 29% of Fortune 500 and approximately 19% of Global 2000 companies are live, paying customers of a leading AI startup, representing real deployments rather than just signed deals.",
      "anchor": "29% of Fortune 500 and ~19% of Global 2000 are live, paying customers of a leading AI startup\n- These are real deployments — top-down contracts, converted pilots, and active usage — not just signed deals"
    },
    {
      "claim": "Enterprise AI penetration happened faster than any prior enterprise technology wave, with large enterprises betting on newer AI products earlier than they ever have before.",
      "anchor": "This penetration happened faster than any prior enterprise technology wave; large enterprises are betting on newer AI products earlier than they ever have before"
    },
    {
      "claim": "Coding is the dominant enterprise AI use case by an order of magnitude, delivering 10-20x productivity gains for engineers with tight human-in-the-loop and verifiable output.",
      "anchor": "Coding — Dominant by an order of magnitude. 10-20x productivity gains for engineers. Tight human-in-the-loop. Verifiable output. Tools: Cursor, Claude Code, Codex."
    },
    {
      "claim": "Support and search are the second and third most adopted enterprise AI use cases, with support featuring high volume, well-defined tasks, and easy-to-test ROI, while search focuses on internal knowledge retrieval and industry-specific applications.",
      "anchor": "Support — High volume, well-defined tasks, clear SOPs, verifiable resolution. Easy to A/B test ROI. Low change management (often outsourced to BPOs already).\n3. **Search** — Internal knowledge retrieval, industry-specific search. Glean (enterprise), Harvey (legal search), OpenEvidence (medical search)."
    },
    {
      "claim": "Legal is a surprising first-mover industry for AI adoption, with Harvey achieving approximately $200M ARR in 3 years, because AI excels at parsing dense text, reasoning over contracts, and summarizing.",
      "anchor": "Legal — Surprising first mover. AI is excellent at parsing dense text, reasoning over contracts, summarizing. Harvey: ~$200M ARR in 3 years."
    },
    {
      "claim": "The most successful AI adoption sectors share characteristics of text-based work, rote and repetitive tasks, natural human-in-the-loop oversight, limited regulation, and verifiable outputs, while industries requiring physical world interaction or heavy regulation are lagging.",
      "anchor": "Text-based work, rote and repetitive tasks, natural human-in-the-loop oversight, limited regulation, verifiable outputs. Industries requiring physical world interaction, interpersonal relationships, or heavy regulation are lagging."
    },
    {
      "claim": "Harvey AI achieved $0 to $200M+ ARR in 36 months with an approximately $11B valuation, founded by Winston Weinberg (securities litigator) and Gabriel Pereyra (DeepMind researcher), with OpenAI as seed investor.",
      "anchor": "Harvey ($0 → $200M+ ARR in 36 months; ~$11B valuation) is the canonical vertical AI success story. Founders: Winston Weinberg (securities litigator) + Gabriel Pereyra (DeepMind researcher). Seed investor: OpenAI (led $5M round in 2022)."
    },
    {
      "claim": "Legal AI works because the billable-hour model provides built-in human-in-the-loop review, legal work is structured into approximately 10 categories, process data exists only inside law firms creating a genuine moat, and prestige signals trust in professional services.",
      "anchor": "Billable-hour model means there's already a human-in-the-loop review system — minimum viable AI quality is lower\n- Legal work is structured: ~10 categories (drafting, document comparison, case law research, etc.)\n- Process data exists only inside law firms — no public training data, so proprietary data is a genuine moat\n- Prestige signals trust in professional services — top firm adoption creates downstream credibility"
    },
    {
      "claim": "Harvey made a contrarian customer choice by going to Allen & Overy (now A&O Shearman, 4,000 lawyers) when they had only 4 employees, establishing that elite firm trust creates downstream credibility.",
      "anchor": "Contrarian customer choice — Went to Allen & Overy (now A&O Shearman, 4,000 lawyers) when they had 4 employees. If elite firms trust you, everyone downstream trusts you."
    },
    {
      "claim": "Harvey uses hyper-personalized demos by researching specific partners, finding their public case filings, and having Harvey analyze their own work to let lawyers argue with the model.",
      "anchor": "Hyper-personalized demos — Before every pitch, researched the specific partner, found their public case filings, had Harvey analyze their own work. \"Upload their own argument. Tell me how to argue against it. Lawyers are argumentative. Let them fight with the model.\""
    },
    {
      "claim": "Harvey's first 50 enterprise customers were all referrals through an external trust flywheel where law firms bring Harvey to their clients, particularly PE funds and their associated law firms.",
      "anchor": "External trust flywheel — Law firms bring Harvey to their clients. PE fund's law firm builds a workflow → PE fund wants it → PE fund pulls their other law firms. First 50 enterprise customers were all referrals."
    },
    {
      "claim": "Harvey first built specialized systems for each legal task type, then unified them into a single interface where the AI routes users via nudges, executing a classic platform move.",
      "anchor": "Expand then collapse — First built specialized systems for each legal task type (separate system for drafting vs. due diligence vs. case research). Then unified into single interface where the AI routes users via \"nudges.\" Classic platform move."
    },
    {
      "claim": "Harvey focuses on gross revenue retention, with median seat count doubling within 12 months of deployment, arguing that many investors mistakenly focus only on net new ARR.",
      "anchor": "GRR focus — \"A lot of investors have been basically just looking at net new ARR... that's a huge mistake.\" Median seat count doubles within 12 months of deployment."
    },
    {
      "claim": "Approximately 20% of Harvey's 460 employees are lawyers, with domain experts crucial for closing enterprise deals and defining product workflows with 15 sub-tasks.",
      "anchor": "Domain experts, not just engineers — ~20% of 460 employees are lawyers. Domain hires: closing enterprise deals (ex-Wachtell partner calling = meeting gets taken), and product design (lawyers define 15 sub-tasks in a workflow, what \"good\" looks like)."
    },
    {
      "claim": "Harvey uses a revenue-share model where they build custom workflows with law firms who then sell to clients, tapping into professional services budgets (billions) rather than tech budgets (millions).",
      "anchor": "Selling work, not just software — Revenue-share model where Harvey builds custom workflows with law firms, law firms sell to clients. Budget comes from professional services spend (billions) not tech budget (millions)."
    },
    {
      "claim": "Harvey runs approximately 6 foundation models (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral) with an orchestration layer that selects the best per task, with 40% of their engineering/product/design team being senior infrastructure engineers.",
      "anchor": "Runs ~6 foundation models (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral) with an orchestration layer that picks the best per task\n- Proprietary process data from inside law firms sits on top\n- 40% of engineering/product/design team is senior infrastructure engineers (moat is the system, not the prompt)"
    },
    {
      "claim": "Harvey initially built a custom-trained case law model preferred by lawyers 97% of the time over GPT-4, driving growth to $190M ARR by January 2026 and $11B valuation by March 2026, but then scrapped it when frontier reasoning models started outperforming it.",
      "anchor": "Harvey initially built a custom-trained case law model in partnership with OpenAI. Lawyers preferred it over GPT-4 97% of the time. The model drove rapid growth: $190M ARR by January 2026, $11B valuation by March 2026, majority of AmLaw 100 as clients.\n\nThen Harvey scrapped the model.\n\nFrontier reasoning models from Google, xAI, OpenAI, and Anthropic started outperforming Harvey's custom legal model on Harvey's own BigLaw Bench evaluation."
    },
    {
      "claim": "Fine-tuning wins decisively only when query patterns are genuinely specialized and underrepresented in general training data, the consequence of errors is high enough to justify sustained investment, and the company has enough distribution to generate meaningful proprietary feedback.",
      "anchor": "Fine-tuning wins decisively only when:\n- Query patterns are genuinely specialized and underrepresented in general training data\n- Consequence of errors is high enough to justify sustained investment\n- Company has enough distribution to generate meaningful proprietary feedback"
    },
    {
      "claim": "Intercom's fin-cx-retrieval custom retrieval model works because customer service reasoning is structurally different from general language tasks, and 40M+ resolved conversations have compounded the advantage.",
      "anchor": "Intercom's fin-cx-retrieval (custom retrieval model for customer service) works because customer service reasoning is structurally different from general language tasks, and 40M+ resolved conversations have compounded the advantage."
    },
    {
      "claim": "Cursor launched Composer 2 in April 2026, a proprietary coding model built on Moonshot AI's Kimi K2.5 that scored 61.7% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, beating Claude Opus 4.6's 58.0% at one-tenth the price.",
      "anchor": "Cursor launched Composer 2 (Apr 2026) — a proprietary coding model built on Moonshot AI's Kimi K2.5 with their own continued pre-training and RL. Scored 61.7% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, beating Claude Opus 4.6 (58.0%), at $0.50/M input tokens (1/10th of Anthropic's flagship)."
    },
    {
      "claim": "The 1-1-1 playbook framework distilled from Mark Cuban's AI agent thesis states that success comes from pointing to one painful workflow inside one business and fixing it fast, rather than picking a vertical too broad.",
      "anchor": "Alton Syn distills Mark Cuban's AI agent thesis into a sharper framework: the money isn't in \"selling AI\" — it's in pointing to **one painful workflow inside one business** and fixing it fast.\n\n**The framework:** One vertical. One workflow. One painful problem. Everything else is distribution.\n\n**Why most people fail:** They pick a vertical too broad (\"healthcare,\" \"real estate,\" \"legal\")."
    },
    {
      "claim": "Clients buy the disappearance of a painful operational loop rather than agents, AI, or technology stacks, with a strong offer making sense to a business owner in under 15 seconds.",
      "anchor": "What clients actually buy:** Not agents, not AI, not your stack — they buy the disappearance of a painful operational loop. A strong offer makes sense to a tired business owner in under 15 seconds: \"when a job is cancelled, the next best customer gets contacted automatically.\""
    },
    {
      "claim": "The real moat in vertical AI is workflow fluency—knowing one painful loop well enough to instantly answer questions about triggers, data needs, urgency criteria, human intervention points, failure modes, and ROI speed.",
      "anchor": "The real moat — workflow fluency:** Knowing one painful loop well enough to instantly answer: What triggers it? What data does it need? What counts as urgent? Where does a human still step in? What breaks most often? What ROI shows up fastest?"
    },
    {
      "claim": "Value is shifting away from node-dragging and manual debugging as a billing model toward workflow selection, commercial packaging, deployment confidence, monitoring, and iteration speed.",
      "anchor": "Where value is shifting:** Away from node-dragging, repetitive setup, and manual debugging as a billing model. Toward workflow selection, commercial packaging, deployment confidence, monitoring, and iteration speed."
    }
  ]
}
```
